  1. Regression Analysis

  3. Week 7

Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA

Research Analysis / Applied Statistics

1 Key Topics

This week covered two main areas:

  • Multiple Comparisons — how to fairly compare more than two group means after a significant ANOVA result
  • Two-Way ANOVA — extending one-way ANOVA to handle two independent variables simultaneously, including their interaction effect
  • Factorial Design — the correct experimental design when testing for interactions
  • Effect Size — measuring how much of the variance is explained by each factor (partial η²)
  • Model Validation in R — checking ANOVA assumptions with diagnostic plots
  • Lab Practice — running one-way ANOVA by hand in Excel and in R, including post-hoc tests

2 Core Explanations

2.1 The Multiple Comparisons Problem

When ANOVA tells us that at least one group mean is different, it does not tell us which groups differ. A natural first instinct is to run separate t-tests for every pair of groups — but this creates a serious problem.

Every individual test carries a risk (α, usually 5%) of a Type I error (falsely concluding there is a difference). When you run many tests on the same data, those error risks stack up. This inflated overall error rate is called the Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER).

Example: Testing 3 soil types (Sand, Clay, Loam) means 3 pairwise comparisons.
With 6 cities (from the New Car Interest Rate dataset), there are 15 pairwise comparisons — the chance of at least one false positive becomes very large.

2.1.1 Post-Hoc Tests

Post-hoc (after-the-fact) tests are designed to run all pairwise comparisons while controlling the FWER at the chosen α level. They are more conservative than plain t-tests.

Method How it works When to use
Tukey’s HSD (Honest Significant Difference) Controls FWER by adjusting critical values Most common; good general choice
Bonferroni Divides α by the number of comparisons Simple; conservative with many comparisons
Separate t-tests No adjustment Avoid — inflated Type I error

2.1.2 Worksheet 4 Results (Soil Yield Data)

Comparison Separate t-tests Tukey’s HSD Bonferroni
Loam – Clay 0.081 0.179 0.234
Loam – Sand 0.006 0.020 0.023
Clay – Sand 0.351 0.555 0.914

Key takeaway: Only the Loam vs. Sand comparison is statistically significant.
Notice how the adjusted p-values (Tukey, Bonferroni) are larger than the raw t-test p-values — this is the conservative adjustment at work.

2.2 Two-Way ANOVA

2.2.1 What is it?

One-way ANOVA tested one independent variable (e.g., soil type) against one continuous dependent variable (yield).

Two-way ANOVA tests two independent (categorical) variables simultaneously against one continuous dependent variable. This is powerful because it also reveals whether the two factors interact.

2.2.2 Main Effects vs. Interaction Effect

  • Main effect of Factor A — the overall effect of Factor A on the response, ignoring Factor B.
  • Main effect of Factor B — the overall effect of Factor B on the response, ignoring Factor A.
  • Interaction effect (A × B) — does the effect of Factor A depend on which level of Factor B we’re at? This is the most important question in two-way ANOVA.

Always check the interaction effect first. If it is significant, the main effects cannot be interpreted in isolation — you must look at them within each level of the other factor (called simple effects).

2.2.3 The Three Null Hypotheses

For a two-way ANOVA with factors A and B:

  1. H₀ (Factor A): All population means for Factor A are equal (no main effect of A)
  2. H₀ (Factor B): All population means for Factor B are equal (no main effect of B)
  3. H₀ (Interaction): The effect of Factor A is the same at all levels of Factor B (no interaction)

Thinking further: A three-way ANOVA has 7 hypotheses — 3 main effects + 3 two-way interactions + 1 three-way interaction.

2.2.4 The Two-Way ANOVA Effects Model

\[Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}\]

Symbol Meaning
\(Y_{ijk}\) Response variable (observation \(k\) at level \(i\) of A and level \(j\) of B)
\(\mu\) Overall population mean
\(\alpha_i\) Treatment effect of Factor A at level \(i\)
\(\beta_j\) Treatment effect of Factor B at level \(j\)
\((\alpha\beta)_{ij}\) Interaction effect
\(\varepsilon_{ijk}\) Residual / error term
\(k\) Replicate index

2.3 Factorial Design

To test for an interaction effect, you must use a factorial (fully crossed) design:

“All possible combinations of all treatment levels must be included for every replicate.”

If Factor A has a levels and Factor B has b levels:

\[\text{Number of treatment combinations} = a \times b\]

With \(r\) replicates:

\[\text{Total observations} = r \times a \times b\]

Example (Viscosity study):
pH has 2 levels (5.6, 5.9) × Catalyst concentration has 2 levels (2.5, 2.7) = 4 combinations.
With 3 replicates → 12 total viscosity measurements.

2.4 Interaction Plots

An interaction plot graphs the mean response for each combination of factor levels. It is the clearest way to visually assess whether an interaction exists.

Plot appearance Conclusion
Lines are approximately parallel No interaction between factors
Lines cross or diverge sharply Interaction is likely present

Tip: Don’t overcomplicate it. Pick one line, trace how the response changes as the other factor changes — then repeat for the other line. If the direction or magnitude of change differs between lines, that’s an interaction.

2.5 Partitioning Variance in Two-Way ANOVA

Total variability is split into components:

\[SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_{Error}\]

The ANOVA table for two-way:

Source SS df MS = SS/df F = MS / MSₑ
Factor A \(SS_A\) \(a - 1\) \(MS_A\) \(F_A\)
Factor B \(SS_B\) \(b - 1\) \(MS_B\) \(F_B\)
Interaction A×B \(SS_{AB}\) \((a-1)(b-1)\) \(MS_{AB}\) \(F_{AB}\)
Error \(SS_E\) \(ab(r-1)\) \(MS_E\) —
Total \(SS_T\) \(abr - 1\) — —

A p-value (or critical F value) is then used to make the decision for each row.

2.6 Significance Stars in R Output

R uses shorthand to indicate significance levels:

Symbol p-value range Interpretation
(blank) > 0.1 Not significant
. 0.05 – 0.1 Marginal / weak
* 0.01 – 0.05 Significant at 5%
** 0.001 – 0.01 Significant at 1%
*** < 0.001 Highly significant

2.7 Simple Effects (Post-Hoc for Two-Way ANOVA)

If the interaction is significant, examine simple effects: the effect of one factor while holding the other factor constant at each level.

Viscosity Example: The interaction was significant (p = 0.03).
- Effect of pH (5.6 vs 5.9) when catalyst = 2.5: not significant
- Effect of pH (5.6 vs 5.9) when catalyst = 2.7: significant (difference = 15, p = 0.01)

2.8 Effect Size — Partial Eta Squared (η²ₚ)

To measure how much of the variance is explained by each factor:

\[\eta^2_p = \frac{SS_{factor}}{SS_{factor} + SS_{error}}\]

This is analogous to R² in regression. It tells you the proportion of variance in the response explained by a given factor, after removing variance accounted for by the other variables.

Interpretation example: η²ₚ = 0.33 for pH means 33% of the variation in viscosity is associated with pH.

2.9 ANOVA Assumptions (Checklist)

Before trusting ANOVA results, verify:

  1. Random sampling — ensured at data collection stage
  2. Equal variances (homogeneity) — test with Levene’s test or Bartlett’s test
  3. Independence of error terms — observations should not influence each other
  4. Normality of residuals — check with QQ-plot and/or Shapiro-Wilk test

If equal variance assumption is violated → use the non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis test (uses ranks/medians instead of means).

3 Key Equations / Formulas

# --- ONE-WAY ANOVA (recap) ---
model <- aov(frame$yield ~ frame$soil)
summary(model)

# --- POST-HOC TESTS ---
TukeyHSD(model)                                          # Tukey's HSD
pairwise.t.test(frame$yield, frame$soil, "bonferroni")   # Bonferroni

# --- TWO-WAY ANOVA ---
model2 <- aov(viscosity ~ pH * catalyst, data = df)
summary(model2)

# --- INTERACTION PLOT ---
interaction.plot(df$pH, df$catalyst, df$viscosity)

# --- EFFECT SIZE (partial eta squared) ---
# SS_factor / (SS_factor + SS_error)
# Can use: library(effectsize); eta_squared(model2, partial = TRUE)

# --- MODEL VALIDATION PLOTS ---
plot(model)   # Produces 4 diagnostic plots

# --- OUTLIER TEST ---
library(outliers)
grubbs.test(clay_data)   # Grubbs test (max normalised residual)

# --- VARIANCE TESTS ---
bartlett.test(yield ~ soil, data = frame)
leveneTest(yield ~ soil, data = frame)   # library(car)

# --- NON-PARAMETRIC ALTERNATIVE ---
kruskal.test(yield ~ soil, data = frame)

Two-Way ANOVA Effects Model:

\[Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}\]

Partial Eta Squared:

\[\eta^2_p = \frac{SS_{factor}}{SS_{factor} + SS_{Error}}\]

Total observations in a factorial design:

\[N = r \times a \times b\]

4 Practical Examples

4.1 Example 1: Soil Yield — One-Way ANOVA + Post-Hoc (Worksheet 4 / Lab)

Setup: Crop yields measured for 10 fields on each of 3 soil types (Sand, Clay, Loam).
Group means: Sand = 9.9, Clay = 11.5, Loam = 14.3
ANOVA p-value: 0.025 → significant (at least one mean differs)

Post-hoc conclusion: Only Loam vs. Sand is significantly different (Tukey p ≈ 0.020, Bonferroni p ≈ 0.023). Clay is not significantly different from either.

4.2 Example 2: Viscosity Study — Two-Way ANOVA with Interaction

Factors: - Factor A = pH (2 levels: 5.6, 5.9) - Factor B = Catalyst Concentration (2 levels: 2.5, 2.7) - 3 replicates → 12 total measurements

R Output Interpretation:

Effect p-value Conclusion
pH (main) > 0.05 Not significant alone
Catalyst (main) > 0.05 Not significant alone
pH × Catalyst (interaction) 0.03 Significant ✓

Because the interaction is significant:
→ The main effects are not meaningful in isolation
→ Must examine simple effects (e.g., effect of pH when catalyst is held at 2.5 vs. 2.7)
→ When catalyst = 2.7, changing pH from 5.6 to 5.9 produces a statistically significant difference (p = 0.01)

4.3 Example 3: New Car Interest Rate (Worksheet 4, Part 2)

Setup: 1 factor (city) with 6 levels → 15 pairwise comparisons
Task: Run one-way ANOVA, then compare Separate t-tests vs. Tukey vs. Bonferroni across all 15 pairs.

Purpose: To show how the multiple comparisons problem scales with more groups — with 15 tests, the chance of a false positive grows substantially if uncorrected p-values are used.

5 Summary Points

  • Multiple comparisons tests (Tukey’s HSD, Bonferroni) control the Family-Wise Error Rate; they are more conservative than raw pairwise t-tests — this is intentional and correct.

  • In two-way ANOVA, always check the interaction effect first. If significant, the main effects cannot be interpreted on their own.

  • An interaction means the effect of one factor depends on the level of the other factor. Interaction plots make this visible: crossing lines = interaction.

  • A factorial design is required to detect interactions — every combination of factor levels must appear in the data.

  • Partial η² measures effect size for two-way ANOVA; interpreted like R² (proportion of variance explained).

  • Before running ANOVA: check equal variances (Levene’s test) and normality of residuals (QQ-plot / Shapiro-Wilk). If variances are unequal, consider Kruskal-Wallis.

  • In R, the aov() function fits ANOVA models; TukeyHSD() and pairwise.t.test(..., "bonferroni") run post-hoc tests; plot(model) produces four validation plots.

6 Annotations & Notes

Questions to revisit:

End of Week 7 Study Guide

Week 6
Week 8
Source Code
---
title: "Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA"
subtitle: "Research Analysis / Applied Statistics"
---

<!-- ============================================================
     STUDY GUIDE — WEEK 7
     Topics: Multiple Comparisons, Two-Way ANOVA, Factorial Design
     Source: Lecture transcript + Worksheet 4
     ============================================================ -->

# Key Topics

This week covered two main areas:

- **Multiple Comparisons** — how to fairly compare more than two group means after a significant ANOVA result
- **Two-Way ANOVA** — extending one-way ANOVA to handle *two* independent variables simultaneously, including their **interaction effect**
- **Factorial Design** — the correct experimental design when testing for interactions
- **Effect Size** — measuring how much of the variance is explained by each factor (partial η²)
- **Model Validation in R** — checking ANOVA assumptions with diagnostic plots
- **Lab Practice** — running one-way ANOVA by hand in Excel and in R, including post-hoc tests

---

# Core Explanations

## The Multiple Comparisons Problem

When ANOVA tells us that *at least one* group mean is different, it does **not** tell us *which* groups differ. A natural first instinct is to run separate t-tests for every pair of groups — but this creates a serious problem.

Every individual test carries a risk (α, usually 5%) of a **Type I error** (falsely concluding there is a difference). When you run many tests on the same data, those error risks stack up. This inflated overall error rate is called the **Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER)**.

> **Example:** Testing 3 soil types (Sand, Clay, Loam) means 3 pairwise comparisons.  
> With 6 cities (from the New Car Interest Rate dataset), there are **15** pairwise comparisons — the chance of at least one false positive becomes very large.

### Post-Hoc Tests

Post-hoc (after-the-fact) tests are designed to run all pairwise comparisons while *controlling* the FWER at the chosen α level. They are more **conservative** than plain t-tests.

| Method | How it works | When to use |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Tukey's HSD** (Honest Significant Difference) | Controls FWER by adjusting critical values | Most common; good general choice |
| **Bonferroni** | Divides α by the number of comparisons | Simple; conservative with many comparisons |
| Separate t-tests | No adjustment | **Avoid** — inflated Type I error |

### Worksheet 4 Results (Soil Yield Data)

| Comparison | Separate t-tests | Tukey's HSD | Bonferroni |
|------------|-----------------|-------------|------------|
| Loam – Clay | 0.081 | 0.179 | 0.234 |
| **Loam – Sand** | **0.006** | **0.020** | **0.023** |
| Clay – Sand | 0.351 | 0.555 | 0.914 |

> **Key takeaway:** Only the Loam vs. Sand comparison is statistically significant.  
> Notice how the adjusted p-values (Tukey, Bonferroni) are *larger* than the raw t-test p-values — this is the conservative adjustment at work.

---

## Two-Way ANOVA

### What is it?

**One-way ANOVA** tested one independent variable (e.g., soil type) against one continuous dependent variable (yield).

**Two-way ANOVA** tests **two** independent (categorical) variables *simultaneously* against one continuous dependent variable. This is powerful because it also reveals whether the two factors **interact**.

### Main Effects vs. Interaction Effect

- **Main effect of Factor A** — the overall effect of Factor A on the response, *ignoring* Factor B.
- **Main effect of Factor B** — the overall effect of Factor B on the response, *ignoring* Factor A.
- **Interaction effect (A × B)** — does the effect of Factor A *depend on which level of Factor B we're at*? This is the most important question in two-way ANOVA.

> **Always check the interaction effect first.** If it is significant, the main effects cannot be interpreted in isolation — you must look at them *within each level of the other factor* (called **simple effects**).

### The Three Null Hypotheses

For a two-way ANOVA with factors A and B:

1. **H₀ (Factor A):** All population means for Factor A are equal (no main effect of A)
2. **H₀ (Factor B):** All population means for Factor B are equal (no main effect of B)
3. **H₀ (Interaction):** The effect of Factor A is the same at all levels of Factor B (no interaction)

> **Thinking further:** A *three-way* ANOVA has **7** hypotheses — 3 main effects + 3 two-way interactions + 1 three-way interaction.

### The Two-Way ANOVA Effects Model

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $Y_{ijk}$ | Response variable (observation $k$ at level $i$ of A and level $j$ of B) |
| $\mu$ | Overall population mean |
| $\alpha_i$ | Treatment effect of Factor A at level $i$ |
| $\beta_j$ | Treatment effect of Factor B at level $j$ |
| $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ | Interaction effect |
| $\varepsilon_{ijk}$ | Residual / error term |
| $k$ | Replicate index |

---

## Factorial Design

To test for an interaction effect, you must use a **factorial (fully crossed) design**:

> *"All possible combinations of all treatment levels must be included for every replicate."*

If Factor A has **a** levels and Factor B has **b** levels:

$$\text{Number of treatment combinations} = a \times b$$

With $r$ replicates:

$$\text{Total observations} = r \times a \times b$$

> **Example (Viscosity study):**  
> pH has 2 levels (5.6, 5.9) × Catalyst concentration has 2 levels (2.5, 2.7) = **4 combinations**.  
> With 3 replicates → **12 total viscosity measurements**.

---

## Interaction Plots

An **interaction plot** graphs the mean response for each combination of factor levels. It is the clearest way to visually assess whether an interaction exists.

| Plot appearance | Conclusion |
|----------------|-----------|
| Lines are approximately **parallel** | No interaction between factors |
| Lines **cross** or diverge sharply | Interaction is likely present |

> **Tip:** Don't overcomplicate it. Pick one line, trace how the response changes as the other factor changes — then repeat for the other line. If the *direction or magnitude* of change differs between lines, that's an interaction.

---

## Partitioning Variance in Two-Way ANOVA

Total variability is split into components:

$$SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_{Error}$$

The **ANOVA table** for two-way:

| Source | SS | df | MS = SS/df | F = MS / MSₑ |
|--------|----|----|-----------|-------------|
| Factor A | $SS_A$ | $a - 1$ | $MS_A$ | $F_A$ |
| Factor B | $SS_B$ | $b - 1$ | $MS_B$ | $F_B$ |
| Interaction A×B | $SS_{AB}$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ | $MS_{AB}$ | $F_{AB}$ |
| Error | $SS_E$ | $ab(r-1)$ | $MS_E$ | — |
| **Total** | $SS_T$ | $abr - 1$ | — | — |

A **p-value** (or critical F value) is then used to make the decision for each row.

---

## Significance Stars in R Output

R uses shorthand to indicate significance levels:

| Symbol | p-value range | Interpretation |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| (blank) | > 0.1 | Not significant |
| `.` | 0.05 – 0.1 | Marginal / weak |
| `*` | 0.01 – 0.05 | Significant at 5% |
| `**` | 0.001 – 0.01 | Significant at 1% |
| `***` | < 0.001 | Highly significant |

---

## Simple Effects (Post-Hoc for Two-Way ANOVA)

If the interaction is significant, examine **simple effects**: the effect of one factor while **holding the other factor constant** at each level.

> **Viscosity Example:** The interaction was significant (p = 0.03).  
> - Effect of pH (5.6 vs 5.9) *when catalyst = 2.5*: not significant  
> - Effect of pH (5.6 vs 5.9) *when catalyst = 2.7*: **significant** (difference = 15, p = 0.01)

---

## Effect Size — Partial Eta Squared (η²ₚ)

To measure *how much* of the variance is explained by each factor:

$$\eta^2_p = \frac{SS_{factor}}{SS_{factor} + SS_{error}}$$

This is analogous to R² in regression. It tells you the proportion of variance in the response explained by a given factor, *after removing variance accounted for by the other variables*.

> **Interpretation example:** η²ₚ = 0.33 for pH means *33% of the variation in viscosity* is associated with pH.

---

## ANOVA Assumptions (Checklist)

Before trusting ANOVA results, verify:

1. **Random sampling** — ensured at data collection stage
2. **Equal variances** (homogeneity) — test with Levene's test or Bartlett's test
3. **Independence of error terms** — observations should not influence each other
4. **Normality of residuals** — check with QQ-plot and/or Shapiro-Wilk test

> If equal variance assumption is violated → use the non-parametric **Kruskal-Wallis test** (uses ranks/medians instead of means).

---

# Key Equations / Formulas

```r
# --- ONE-WAY ANOVA (recap) ---
model <- aov(frame$yield ~ frame$soil)
summary(model)

# --- POST-HOC TESTS ---
TukeyHSD(model)                                          # Tukey's HSD
pairwise.t.test(frame$yield, frame$soil, "bonferroni")   # Bonferroni

# --- TWO-WAY ANOVA ---
model2 <- aov(viscosity ~ pH * catalyst, data = df)
summary(model2)

# --- INTERACTION PLOT ---
interaction.plot(df$pH, df$catalyst, df$viscosity)

# --- EFFECT SIZE (partial eta squared) ---
# SS_factor / (SS_factor + SS_error)
# Can use: library(effectsize); eta_squared(model2, partial = TRUE)

# --- MODEL VALIDATION PLOTS ---
plot(model)   # Produces 4 diagnostic plots

# --- OUTLIER TEST ---
library(outliers)
grubbs.test(clay_data)   # Grubbs test (max normalised residual)

# --- VARIANCE TESTS ---
bartlett.test(yield ~ soil, data = frame)
leveneTest(yield ~ soil, data = frame)   # library(car)

# --- NON-PARAMETRIC ALTERNATIVE ---
kruskal.test(yield ~ soil, data = frame)
```

**Two-Way ANOVA Effects Model:**

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

**Partial Eta Squared:**

$$\eta^2_p = \frac{SS_{factor}}{SS_{factor} + SS_{Error}}$$

**Total observations in a factorial design:**

$$N = r \times a \times b$$

---

# Practical Examples

## Example 1: Soil Yield — One-Way ANOVA + Post-Hoc (Worksheet 4 / Lab)

**Setup:** Crop yields measured for 10 fields on each of 3 soil types (Sand, Clay, Loam).  
**Group means:** Sand = 9.9, Clay = 11.5, Loam = 14.3  
**ANOVA p-value:** 0.025 → significant (at least one mean differs)

**Post-hoc conclusion:** Only **Loam vs. Sand** is significantly different (Tukey p ≈ 0.020, Bonferroni p ≈ 0.023). Clay is not significantly different from either.

---

## Example 2: Viscosity Study — Two-Way ANOVA with Interaction

**Factors:**
- Factor A = pH (2 levels: 5.6, 5.9)
- Factor B = Catalyst Concentration (2 levels: 2.5, 2.7)
- 3 replicates → 12 total measurements

**R Output Interpretation:**

| Effect | p-value | Conclusion |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| pH (main) | > 0.05 | Not significant alone |
| Catalyst (main) | > 0.05 | Not significant alone |
| **pH × Catalyst (interaction)** | **0.03** | **Significant** ✓ |

**Because the interaction is significant:**  
→ The main effects are *not meaningful in isolation*  
→ Must examine simple effects (e.g., effect of pH when catalyst is held at 2.5 vs. 2.7)  
→ When catalyst = 2.7, changing pH from 5.6 to 5.9 produces a statistically significant difference (p = 0.01)

---

## Example 3: New Car Interest Rate (Worksheet 4, Part 2)

**Setup:** 1 factor (city) with **6 levels** → 15 pairwise comparisons  
**Task:** Run one-way ANOVA, then compare Separate t-tests vs. Tukey vs. Bonferroni across all 15 pairs.

> **Purpose:** To show how the multiple comparisons problem *scales* with more groups — with 15 tests, the chance of a false positive grows substantially if uncorrected p-values are used.

---

# Summary Points

- **Multiple comparisons tests** (Tukey's HSD, Bonferroni) control the Family-Wise Error Rate; they are more conservative than raw pairwise t-tests — this is intentional and correct.

- In **two-way ANOVA**, always check the **interaction effect first**. If significant, the main effects cannot be interpreted on their own.

- An **interaction** means the effect of one factor *depends* on the level of the other factor. Interaction plots make this visible: **crossing lines = interaction**.

- A **factorial design** is required to detect interactions — every combination of factor levels must appear in the data.

- **Partial η²** measures effect size for two-way ANOVA; interpreted like R² (proportion of variance explained).

- Before running ANOVA: check **equal variances** (Levene's test) and **normality of residuals** (QQ-plot / Shapiro-Wilk). If variances are unequal, consider **Kruskal-Wallis**.

- In R, the `aov()` function fits ANOVA models; `TukeyHSD()` and `pairwise.t.test(..., "bonferroni")` run post-hoc tests; `plot(model)` produces four validation plots.

---

# Annotations & Notes

<!-- Space below for your own notes, questions, or additional examples -->

**Questions to revisit:**

- [ ] Why does Levene's test require normality? When is Fligner-Killeen more appropriate?
- [ ] How does the choice of reference (baseline) group in R affect coefficient interpretation?
- [ ] Practice calculating SS_A, SS_B, SS_AB, and SS_Error by hand for the viscosity dataset.
- [ ] What exactly does the Grubbs test statistic measure? Look up the formula.
- [ ] Complete Worksheet 4, Part 2 (New Car Interest Rate): fill in the full comparison table.

---

*End of Week 7 Study Guide*
